context: Current closing price of Chevron (CVX): $190.36 USD as of 2026-04-09.
justification_text:
The point estimates at each time horizon were determined by analyzing trends, patterns, and any underlying factors influencing the variable being forecast. In the short term (like one day ahead), the forecast leans heavily on the most recent data, as very little time has passed for any major changes to unfold. The closer time horizon tends to reflect continuity or momentum from current conditions, so the one-day estimate of 190.5 assumes that changes will likely be marginal and driven primarily by near-term dynamics, like daily fluctuations or specific, visible short-term drivers. For the one-week (191.2) and one-month (193.0) forecasts, longer-term trends or forces such as broader market movements, seasonal cycles, or externally driven influences start to have a greater role. The gradual rise in the point estimate hints at an expectation of incremental growth, possibly informed by ongoing trends or expectations of some degree of recovery, growth, or other progress in the system or entity being modeled.

The width of the confidence intervals, however, comes from the inherent uncertainty associated with each time frame. At the shortest span (one day), uncertainty is low because fewer variables have had time to act, and our confidence in the outcomes is higher. As horizons lengthen to one week or one month, the interplay of more unknown factors—such as shifts in market conditions, external shocks, or accumulating unpredictabilities—creates greater room for deviation. This is why the one-month interval is much wider (180.0 to 206.0) compared to the one-day interval (188.6 to 192.4). As time progresses, compounding uncertainties—whether they stem from randomness, changing relationships in the data, or model limitations—naturally increase, making our predictions less precise.

Specific risks or events could potentially push outcomes beyond even the bounds of the wide one-month confidence intervals. Sudden, unexpected shocks—such as economic crises, geopolitical upheavals, natural disasters, or regulatory changes—could significantly alter the trajectory. Similarly, extreme deviations in any driving factor, like a sudden collapse in demand or a breakthrough development, could cause large, abrupt shifts. These risks are hard to fully account for in a model, but they highlight the limits of forecasting and why most models are prone to greater uncertainty when looking further into the future. While the predictions aim to capture a 90% chance of outcomes within the intervals, rare or extreme events fall into the 10% outside—and are always a possibility.
